topics.title: Safe JSON State Persistence — Atomic Write Patterns

strengths: Uses os.replace() (Python 3.3+, atomic on POSIX + Windows)
Temp file in same directory → same filesystem → rename is atomic
Cleanup of temp file on write failure
Works on a snapshot (dict copy) → no side effects on caller's dict
gaps: NO os.fsync() before os.replace() — data may be in OS buffer, not on disk. Power failure after rename can leave corrupt/empty file
Uses hardcoded '.tmp' suffix — two process instances writing simultaneously could collide
No checksum / integrity verification on load() — cannot detect bit rot or partial write
No schema_version in state — no migration path when state shape changes
json.dump uses default allow_nan=True — if NaN/Infinity ever enters state, produces non-spec-compliant JSON

best_practices.name: fsync before rename
best_practices.code: f.flush(); os.fsync(f.fileno())  # after json.dump, before os.replace
best_practices.why: Ensures data hits physical disk before the atomic rename. Without this, a power failure after rename but before OS flushes cache → empty/corrupt file at STATE_PATH.

best_practices.name: tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile for collision-safe temp names
best_practices.code:
with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(mode='w', dir=os.path.dirname(path), delete=False, prefix='.state_', suffix='.json') as tf:
    json.dump(snapshot, tf, ...)
    tf.flush(); os.fsync(tf.fileno())
os.replace(tf.name, path)
best_practices.why: Avoids .tmp collision if two Hermes sessions run simultaneously. tempfile generates unique names.

best_practices.name: checksum + completion flag on save/load
best_practices.why: Detects bit rot, partial writes, truncation. is_complete flag catches mid-write crashes.

best_practices.name: allow_nan=False for spec-compliant JSON
best_practices.why: Prevents NaN/Infinity in state from producing invalid JSON that Go/Rust/JS parsers reject. Fails fast at save time instead of producing undecodable state.

best_practices.name: schema_version + migration on load
best_practices.code:
if record.get("schema_version", 0) < CURRENT_VERSION:
    state = migrate(record["state"], record["schema_version"])
best_practices.why: Allows safe evolution of state shape without breaking existing persisted state.

references: ActiveState recipe 579097 — atomic_write context manager with os.replace + os.fsync
Playbook atomic_write.py — checksum verification pattern (sha256 + is_complete flag)
CPython json docs — allow_nan=False for RFC-compliant output
Agent State Desync article — checkpoint checksum + completion flag pattern

topics.title: Hermes Handler Patterns — Double-Load Bug Analysis in handle_convergence

flow: 1. state = pstate.load()        ← SINGLE load at top
2. Modify state dict in memory (findings, fingerprint, round)
5. If terminal: modify state + pstate.save(state) again
current_state.verdict: NO double-load bug present. The 'state' variable stays alive in memory throughout the handler. Not reloaded after save.

analysis.what_double_load_would_look_like: state = load(); save(state); state = load()  ← redundant and wastes I/O. Not present.
analysis.subtle_concern: pstate.save() works on `snapshot = dict(state)` — a shallow copy. If state dict contained mutable sub-objects (lists, nested dicts) modified after save but before the copy, those mutations would be silently persisted. Not an issue here because save() happens BEFORE further state mutation.
analysis.save_twice_pattern: The handler calls save() twice when terminal decision (converged/stuck/maxed_out): once after findings update, once after status update. This is two writes, not a load-after-save. The in-memory state is correct both times.
analysis.save_order_observation: evaluate_convergence() is called AFTER the first save() — this means the on-disk state already has the new findings+round, but the convergence check correctly operates on the same in-memory state. No inconsistency.

best_practices.name: Single load, single save (when possible)
best_practices.code:
state = pstate.load()          # once
# ... all modifications ...
pstate.save(state)              # once at end
result = evaluate_convergence(state)
pstate.save(state)              # if needed, but keep in-memory sync
best_practices.why: Minimize I/O. The current double-save is harmless but inelegant. Could combine: always save after all modifications, not mid-way.

best_practices.name: fail_open on load failure
best_practices.why: Hermes plugin handlers must never crash the parent agent. Graceful error JSON with decision='unknown' lets the orchestrator decide.

best_practices.name: always return valid JSON in handler
best_practices.code:
except Exception as e:
    return json.dumps({"error": str(e), "traceback": traceback.format_exc()}, ensure_ascii=False)
best_practices.why: Silent exceptions kill pipelines. traceback in error response enables debugging. Already done correctly in all 7 handlers.

best_practices.name: separation: handler logic vs state logic
best_practices.code:
# Handler: orchestrates
# state.py: pure functions (save/load/evaluate_convergence)
# classify.py: pure function (classify)
best_practices.why: Already well-structured. Handlers are thin wrappers. State logic and classification are pure functions, testable without Hermes runtime.

best_practices.name: log to stderr, return JSON to stdout
best_practices.code:
import sys
print(json.dumps({"event": "convergence_check", "round": state['round']}), file=sys.stderr)
return json.dumps(result)  # to stdout → tool return value
best_practices.why: Hermes reads stdout as tool output. Logging should go to stderr. Currently no logging at all — adding structured stderr logging would help debugging without affecting tool output.

references: hermes-plugin-development skill — handler patterns, error handling, 'never crash the parent agent'
LangGraph checkpoint store pattern — checkpoint store is source of truth, not in-memory copy → analogous to state.json being the authority

topics.title: Structlog vs print/logging for Python Plugins

findings.recommendation: For Hermes plugins specifically: use plain print() to stderr for diagnostics, NOT structlog (avoid adding dependency). Tool output goes to stdout (JSON).
rationale: Hermes plugins are loaded into the agent process — adding structlog dependency creates version conflicts with the host's logging configuration
Plugin stdout is captured as tool return value → MUST be clean JSON. Any logging to stdout corrupts tool output.
stderr is the safe channel for plugin diagnostics — visible in gateway logs, doesn't interfere with tool protocol
If structured logging is needed later: structlog configured via structlog.stdlib.LoggerFactory() can coexist with stdlib logging → both flow through same handlers

stdlib_logging.pros: Zero dependencies, thread-safe, dictConfig for YAML-driven setup, every Python dev knows it
stdlib_logging.cons: Unstructured by default, no context propagation (contextvars must be hand-wired), JSON formatter must be written manually

structlog.pros: Structured key-value native, composable processor chain, built-in JSON renderer, contextvars-aware, can wrap stdlib logging
structlog.cons: Additional dependency, must be configured before first log call (cache_logger_on_first_use), overkill for simple plugins

loguru.pros: Single global logger, enqueue=True for non-blocking, colored output, simpler API
loguru.cons: Another dependency, global state (harder to isolate in plugin context), patches stdlib logging on import

findings.current_code_status: No logging in the plugin. All errors returned as JSON in handler responses. This is correct for tool handlers — they should not log to stdout.

best_practice_for_plugins.tool_output: return json.dumps(result)  # to Hermes via stdout
best_practice_for_plugins.error_reporting: return json.dumps({"error": str(e), "traceback": traceback.format_exc()})  # inline in tool response
never: print() to stdout — corrupts tool protocol
add dependency (structlog/loguru) — version conflicts with host

references: structlog.org documentation — processor chain model, stdlib integration
Python Observability deep-dive — stdlib vs structlog vs loguru comparison
hermes-plugin-development skill — 'log upstream API errors with logger.error(...), never log raw API keys'

topics.title: Test Patterns for JSON Serialization Edge Cases (NaN, Infinity in findings)

current_state.problem: state.py:save() uses json.dump with default allow_nan=True. If findings contain float('nan') or float('inf') (e.g., from a scoring calculation), state.json will contain non-standard JSON that Go/Rust/JS parsers reject.

recommended_tests.rationale: Catch NaN before it hits disk. Currently allow_nan=True would silently produce invalid JSON.
recommended_tests.code:
import json, math, pytest

def test_save_rejects_nan(monkeypatch, tmp_path):
    monkeypatch.setattr(state, 'STATE_PATH', str(tmp_path / 'state.json'))
    bad_state = {"findings": [{"severity": "P0", "confidence": float('nan')}]}
    # If allow_nan=False is added to save():
    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
        state.save(bad_state)
    # If allow_nan=True (current default): state.json would contain 'NaN' — invalid JSON

recommended_tests.rationale: Same as NaN — Infinity is not valid JSON.
If state.json was written by old code (allow_nan=True), load() must handle NaN gracefully.
recommended_tests.code:
import json
with open(state.STATE_PATH, 'w') as f:
    f.write('{"findings_fingerprint": "abc", "value": NaN}')  # invalid JSON
result = state.load()
assert result is None  # json.JSONDecodeError → None (current behaviour, correct)

recommended_tests.rationale: Verify integer overflow, empty strings, None, deep nesting survive roundtrip.
recommended_tests.code:
edge_cases = {
    "empty_str": "",
    "null_val": None,
    "zero": 0,
    "negative": -1,
    "large_int": 2**53,  # max safe JS integer
    "deep": {"a": {"b": {"c": [1,2,3]}}},
    "unicode": "\u0000\uffff\U0001f4a9",  # null, max BMP, emoji
    "bool": True,
    "float_special": 0.0,  # -0.0 vs 0.0 (JSON treats both as 0)
}
state.save(edge_cases)
loaded = state.load()
assert loaded["null_val"] is None
assert loaded["unicode"] == edge_cases["unicode"]
assert loaded["large_int"] == 2**53

recommended_tests.rationale: Validate that findings serialization rejects non-finite floats specifically.
recommended_tests.code:
import math
def test_dump_findings_rejects_nonfinite():
    findings = [{"severity": "P0", "file": "x.py", "category": "bug", "numeric_field": math.nan}]
    with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="Out of range float"):
        json.dumps(findings, allow_nan=False)

recommended_tests.rationale: Findings with control chars, emoji, or long descriptions should produce consistent fingerprints.
recommended_tests.code:
import hashlib
def test_fingerprint_unicode_stable():
    a = [{"severity": "P0", "file": "x.py", "category": "bug", "description": "emoji \U0001f4a9 test"}]
    fp1 = state._compute_fingerprint(a)
    fp2 = state._compute_fingerprint(a)
    assert fp1 == fp2  # same input → same hash, regardless of content

python_json_gotchas: json.dumps with indent=None vs indent=2 uses different code paths (C vs Python) → different error messages for allow_nan=False
json.loads('NaN') works in Python but fails in Go/Rust/JS → never write NaN to disk if state is consumed outside Python
float('-0.0') serializes as '-0.0' but loads back as -0.0 — not an issue for most use cases but be aware
json.dumps(Decimal('1.1')) → TypeError — Decimal is not JSON-serializable. Use default=str or convert to float first.
json.loads with parse_constant=lambda s: (_ for _ in ()).throw(ValueError('Invalid')) can reject NaN/Infinity on load
references: Python json docs — allow_nan, parse_constant parameters
CPython issue #98306 — allow_nan='null' / custom NaN serialization (Python 3.14+)
Evan Hahn blog — 'Python's nonstandard JSON encoding' — cross-language compatibility survey
CPython test_json/test_dump.py — official edge case tests

actionable_recommendations.what: Add allow_nan=False to state.save() json.dump call
actionable_recommendations.why: Prevents invalid JSON on disk if NaN/Infinity ever appears in findings. Fail-fast at save time.
actionable_recommendations.impact: Backward compatible for existing valid states. Only non-finite floats trigger ValueError.

actionable_recommendations.what: Add os.fsync() after json.dump before os.replace()
actionable_recommendations.why: Guarantees data hits physical disk before atomic rename. Critical for crash resilience.
actionable_recommendations.code:
with open(tmp_path, 'w', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    json.dump(snapshot, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2, allow_nan=False)
    f.flush()
    os.fsync(f.fileno())
os.replace(tmp_path, state_path)

actionable_recommendations.what: Add edge case tests for JSON serialization (NaN, Infinity, null, unicode, large ints)
actionable_recommendations.why: Current test suite covers happy path only. Missing NaN/Infinity rejection, unicode roundtrip, edge value handling.

actionable_recommendations.what: Use tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile instead of hardcoded .tmp suffix
actionable_recommendations.why: Avoids collision if two Hermes sessions write state simultaneously.

actionable_recommendations.what: Add schema_version to state for future migration support
actionable_recommendations.why: Enables safe evolution of state shape without breaking existing persisted state.

actionable_recommendations.what: Consolidate double-save in handle_convergence into single save at end
actionable_recommendations.why: Minor: reduce I/O. Current double-save is correct but slightly wasteful.
actionable_recommendations.note: Not a bug — in-memory state stays consistent. Purely optimization.

verdict.no_double_load_bug: handle_convergence loads state ONCE and keeps it in memory. Does NOT reload after save(). Correct.
verdict.atomic_write_present: state.py already uses os.replace() pattern. Needs fsync + allow_nan=False hardening.
verdict.no_logging_needed: Current zero-logging approach is correct for plugin tool handlers. Add stderr diagnostics if debug needed.
verdict.test_gaps: Missing NaN/Infinity rejection tests, unicode roundtrip, edge value serialization. 4 new test cases recommended.
